children.name: Alcedo
children.description: Electric jewels of waterways across Eurasia, Africa, and Southeast Asia. The Common Kingfisher's brilliant cobalt-blue and orange plumage is unmistakeable as it darts low over water or plunges from a perch. Despite appearing blue, the feathers contain no blue pigment — the colour is structural, produced by nanostructures diffracting light.

children.name: Alcedo atthis
children.description: A dazzling jewel of clear streams and lake margins, plunging from a perch to seize small fish in its dagger bill. Severe winters that freeze waterways cause sharp population crashes, from which it recovers quickly.

children.name: Alcedo semitorquata
children.description: A striking blue kingfisher of southern and eastern Africa, closely associated with clear fast-flowing rivers and streams. It is near-threatened due to sensitivity to water pollution and habitat degradation.

children.name: Alcedo meninting
children.description: A compact, deep-blue kingfisher of shaded forest streams across South and Southeast Asia. Easily confused with the Common Kingfisher but distinguished by its deep blue ear-coverts and darker underparts.

children.name: Alcedo coerulescens
children.description: A tiny kingfisher endemic to the Indonesian islands of Java, Bali, Lombok, and Sumbawa, favouring coastal mangroves and small streams. Its almost entirely blue-and-white plumage sets it apart from related Alcedo species.

children.name: Alcedo euryzona
children.description: A scarce kingfisher of clear, fast-flowing hill streams in South and Southeast Asia, notable for the broad blue breast-band that gives it its name. It is assessed as vulnerable due to deforestation and stream degradation.

children.name: Alcedo quadribrachys
children.description: A brilliant iridescent-blue kingfisher found along forested rivers and streams of West and Central Africa. It is secretive and most often detected by its sharp piping call as it shoots low over water.

children.name: Alcedo hercules
children.description: The largest of the Alcedo kingfishers, restricted to hill and submontane streams in northeastern India, southern China, and mainland Southeast Asia. It closely resembles the Common Kingfisher but is noticeably larger with a proportionately heavier bill.

children.name: Alcedo websteri
children.description: A little-known, small kingfisher endemic to New Britain and New Ireland in the Bismarck Archipelago of Papua New Guinea, recently elevated to full species status. It inhabits forest streams and is rarely encountered due to its restricted island range.

children.name: Corythornis
children.description: Small, crested African and Malagasy pygmy kingfishers closely associated with rivers, lakes, and wetland margins. These birds show brilliant iridescent plumage and are important indicators of aquatic ecosystem health.

children.name: Corythornis cristatus
children.description: A dazzlingly coloured tiny kingfisher with a barred blue-and-black crest, perching on reeds and aquatic vegetation along rivers, lakes, and marshes throughout sub-Saharan Africa. It plunges vertically for small fish and aquatic insects, often hovering briefly before striking.

children.name: Corythornis leucogaster
children.description: A small forest kingfisher of West and Central African rainforest interiors, distinguished from the Malachite Kingfisher by its pure white underparts. It inhabits shaded, sluggish streams and forest pools, hunting small fish and aquatic invertebrates.

children.name: Corythornis vintsioides
children.description: The common kingfisher of Madagascar and the Comoros, closely resembling the African Malachite Kingfisher but treated as a separate species due to its island isolation and subtle plumage differences. It is widespread along rivers, lakes, and coastal lagoons on Madagascar.

children.name: Corythornis madagascariensis
children.description: A small, forest-dwelling kingfisher endemic to Madagascar, typically found in humid eastern forest away from open water, hunting invertebrates in the understory. Its terrestrial habits and preference for forest interiors distinguish it from other Malagasy kingfishers.

children.name: Corythornis thomensis
children.description: A critically endangered kingfisher endemic to the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea, one of the rarest birds in Africa with fewer than 1,000 individuals estimated. It inhabits primary and secondary forest streams and is threatened by deforestation and introduced predators.

children.name: Ispidina
children.description: African pygmy kingfishers are diminutive, jewel-like birds of sub-Saharan African woodland and forest that, unlike most kingfishers, rarely venture near water. They hunt insects and small lizards in terrestrial habitats and are intra-African migrants across much of their range.

children.name: Ispidina picta
children.description: One of Africa's smallest and most richly coloured birds, a thumb-sized jewel of violet, rufous, and orange found in woodland, bushveld, and forest across sub-Saharan Africa. Surprisingly terrestrial for a kingfisher, it hunts insects, spiders, and small frogs in leaf litter and undergrowth.

children.name: Ispidina lecontei
children.description: Africa's smallest kingfisher, a tiny chestnut-and-blue bird of West African rainforest understory that rarely ventures to water. It hunts small insects and invertebrates on the forest floor, and its diminutive size makes it difficult to observe in the dense vegetation it favours.

children.name: Ceyx
children.description: Small, jewel-like river and forest kingfishers distributed across South and Southeast Asia and Australasia. Many species were long treated as subspecies of the Oriental Dwarf Kingfisher, and recent taxonomy has substantially increased species counts within this genus.

children.name: Ceyx erithaca
children.description: A diminutive gem of tropical forest understory across South and Southeast Asia, with vivid yellow-orange and rufous plumage. It hunts insects, lizards, and small frogs along shaded forest streams and is a seasonal intra-Asian migrant.

children.name: Ceyx rufidorsa
children.description: A brightly coloured dwarf kingfisher of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo, and the Philippines with a distinctive rufous back distinguishing it from the closely related Oriental Dwarf Kingfisher. It inhabits lowland and hill forest streams, hunting invertebrates in the understory.

children.name: Ceyx lepidus
children.description: A variable complex of small dwarf kingfishers from the Moluccan islands and the Philippines, characterised by rufous-and-violet plumage with a white or pale underside. Recent taxonomic revisions have split several island forms from this species, and further splits may follow.

children.name: Ceyx mindanensis
children.description: A small, silvery-blue dwarf kingfisher endemic to Mindanao and Leyte in the southern Philippines, associated with forest streams and rivers. It was split from the Northern Silvery Kingfisher and is threatened by ongoing deforestation.

children.name: Ceyx flumenicola
children.description: A critically endangered tiny kingfisher restricted to Samar, Leyte, and Dinagat Islands in the central Philippines, where it haunts rocky forest streams and rivers. Severe deforestation has reduced its habitat to small fragments, making this one of the most threatened of all kingfishers.

children.name: Ceyx melanurus
children.description: A vibrantly coloured tiny kingfisher of lowland and foothill forests in the Philippines, with an orange-rufous body and violet-blue tail. It is vulnerable to extinction due to rapid deforestation across its island range.

children.name: Ceyx nigromaxilla
children.description: A small dwarf kingfisher of New Guinea and the Bismarck Archipelago, distinguished from related species by its black lower mandible. It inhabits lowland forest streams and is one of several closely related Ceyx kingfishers in New Guinea.

children.name: Ceyx sacerdotis
children.description: A small, brightly coloured dwarf kingfisher endemic to the Sula Islands in the North Moluccas of Indonesia, inhabiting forest interior. Formerly treated as a subspecies of the Moluccan Dwarf Kingfisher, it was elevated to full species status based on morphological and distributional differences.

children.name: Ceyx cajeli
children.description: A little-known dwarf kingfisher endemic to Buru Island in the Moluccas of Indonesia, living in forested areas of this relatively undisturbed island. It was split from the variable Moluccan Dwarf Kingfisher complex based on its restricted range and distinct morphology.

children.name: Ceyx solitarius
children.description: A small, rufous-and-violet dwarf kingfisher endemic to the Solomon Islands archipelago, inhabiting rainforest interiors on several islands. Each island population shows slight plumage variation, and some are treated as separate subspecies.

children.name: Ceyx fallax
children.description: A scarce forest kingfisher endemic to Sulawesi and nearby islands in Indonesia, roosting and nesting in forest-floor burrows. Little studied, it inhabits primary lowland and hill forests and is threatened by ongoing deforestation.

children.name: Ceyx goodfellowi
children.description: A scarce forest kingfisher endemic to Mindanao in the Philippines, notable for its scaly pattern on the breast created by pale feather edging. It inhabits montane forest and is rarely recorded, with very little known about its ecology.

children.name: Ceyx margarethae
children.description: An unusual Philippine dwarf kingfisher in which two distinct colour morphs occur — a rufous morph and a blue-and-white morph — within the same population on certain islands. It inhabits lowland forest on several Philippine islands and is threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Ceyx azureus
children.description: A deep-blue and rufous kingfisher perching quietly on low branches over rivers and lakes across Australia and New Guinea. It dives swiftly for small fish and aquatic invertebrates and is often detected by its high-pitched call.

children.name: Ceyx pusillus
children.description: Australia's smallest kingfisher, barely larger than a wren, haunting mangroves and forested streams in northern Australia and New Guinea. Despite its tiny size it is a capable fish-hunter, darting into the water from overhanging branches.

children.name: Ceyx websteri
children.description: A small dwarf kingfisher endemic to Manus Island in the Admiralty Islands of Papua New Guinea, one of the most restricted-range kingfishers in the Pacific. It inhabits forest and has been very little studied since its original description.

children.name: Ceyx opisthoprocta
children.description: A small dwarf kingfisher from the Sangir Islands of Indonesia, notable for the diagnostic white spot on its rump visible in flight. It was formerly lumped with the Moluccan Dwarf Kingfisher complex but is now treated as a distinct species.

children.name: Ceyx pallidus
children.description: A small dwarf kingfisher of Obi Island in the North Moluccas of Indonesia, distinguished by its pale nape patch from related species in the region. Its remote island location means it is very poorly known.

children.name: Ceyx cyanopectus
children.description: A striking small kingfisher endemic to Luzon and Mindoro in the northern Philippines, with a vivid indigo-blue band across its rufous breast setting it apart from other Philippine Ceyx. It inhabits clear rocky streams in foothill and mountain forest.

children.name: Ceyx argutus
children.description: A small dwarf kingfisher formerly treated as part of the Sulawesi Dwarf Kingfisher complex, restricted to certain islands around Sulawesi. It inhabits forested streams and is rarely observed, with its taxonomy still being clarified by ornithologists.

children.name: Ceyx melanotis
children.description: A small dwarf kingfisher of the Sulu Archipelago and Tawi-Tawi in the southern Philippines, recently separated from the Indigo-banded Kingfisher complex. It inhabits forest streams and is considered vulnerable due to the high rate of deforestation in the Sulu islands.

children.name: Ceyx wallacii
children.description: A little-known dwarf kingfisher endemic to the northern Moluccan islands of Ternate and Tidore in Indonesia, known from very few historical specimens and rarely confirmed in the field. Its precise status relative to other Moluccan Ceyx remains uncertain.

children.name: Dacelo
children.description: Large, terrestrial kingfishers of Australasia colloquially known as kookaburras, famous for their loud laughing calls that echo through eucalypt forests and suburban gardens. Despite their family classification they are primarily carnivores of dry land, eating snakes, lizards, rodents, and large insects.

children.name: Dacelo novaeguineae
children.description: Australia's iconic 'bushman's clock', whose raucous laughing call is one of the most recognisable bird sounds in the world. It is a cooperative breeder in which offspring from previous years help raise younger siblings, living in stable family territories in open woodland and suburban gardens.

children.name: Dacelo leachii
children.description: A large kookaburra of northern Australia and southern New Guinea with striking sky-blue wings and rump and pale eyes giving it an intense stare. Its call is a raucous, cackling laugh similar to but distinct from the Laughing Kookaburra.

children.name: Dacelo gaudichaud
children.description: A medium-large kookaburra endemic to the lowland forests of New Guinea, distinguished from its larger relatives by its warm rufous underparts. It inhabits forest edges and clearings and feeds on large insects, lizards, and small vertebrates.

children.name: Dacelo tyro
children.description: A distinctive kookaburra of the Aru Islands and southern New Guinea, with a heavily spangled blue-and-white plumage pattern unlike the more uniformly coloured mainland kookaburras. It inhabits savanna and open woodland and is less well-studied than its Australian relatives.

children.name: Clytoceyx
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Shovel-billed Kookaburra of New Guinea, a unique forest kingfisher with an exceptionally short, broad, shovel-shaped bill adapted for digging earthworms and other soil invertebrates from the forest floor.

children.name: Clytoceyx rex
children.description: An extraordinary New Guinean forest kingfisher with a uniquely shovel-shaped bill unlike any other member of the family, used to dig earthworms and soil invertebrates from damp forest floors. It is the largest kingfisher in New Guinea and has a distinctive deep, booming call.

children.name: Melidora
children.description: A monotypic genus endemic to New Guinea containing the Hook-billed Kingfisher, an unusual forest kingfisher with a distinctly hooked bill tip adapted for extracting invertebrates from bark and rotting wood. It is crepuscular in habits and rarely observed.

children.name: Melidora macrorrhina
children.description: A medium-sized, chestnut-and-white New Guinean forest kingfisher with a distinctive hook at the tip of its bill, used to extract beetles and other invertebrates from bark and decaying wood. It is crepuscular and largely terrestrial in habits, making it one of the least observed of all kingfishers.

children.name: Tanysiptera
children.description: Paradise kingfishers are exquisite birds of New Guinean and northern Australian rainforests, renowned for the dramatically elongated white central tail feathers of the male that flutter behind in flight. They nest in termite mounds and are among the most sought-after kingfishers by birdwatchers.

children.name: Tanysiptera galatea
children.description: The most widespread paradise kingfisher, found across New Guinea and many surrounding islands with striking blue upperparts and white tail streamers that can exceed the body length. It inhabits lowland and hill forest, excavating nest chambers inside active arboreal termite mounds.

children.name: Tanysiptera sylvia
children.description: A spectacular migratory kingfisher that breeds in the rainforests of Cape York Peninsula, Australia, arriving each October from New Guinea with long white tail streamers streaming behind. It is the only paradise kingfisher known to breed in Australia.

children.name: Tanysiptera hydrocharis
children.description: The smallest paradise kingfisher, endemic to the Aru Islands of Indonesia and the southern lowlands of New Guinea. It shares the brilliant blue-and-white plumage and elongated tail streamers of its relatives but is notably more compact in size.

children.name: Tanysiptera nympha
children.description: A stunning paradise kingfisher endemic to New Guinea with rich orange-red underparts contrasting with deep blue upperparts and long white tail streamers. It inhabits lowland and mid-montane forest and is less commonly encountered than the widespread Common Paradise Kingfisher.

children.name: Tanysiptera danae
children.description: A rare paradise kingfisher endemic to southeastern New Guinea with a distinctive brown-and-rufous head setting it apart from its blue-crowned relatives. Known from very few specimens and sightings, it inhabits lowland forest and is among the least known of all paradise kingfishers.

children.name: Tanysiptera carolinae
children.description: A paradise kingfisher endemic to the small island of Numfor in Geelvink Bay off the north coast of New Guinea, with a very restricted range and little data available on its ecology. Like other paradise kingfishers it sports elongated white tail streamers and brilliant blue upperparts.

children.name: Tanysiptera ellioti
children.description: A small paradise kingfisher endemic to Kofiau Island in the Raja Ampat archipelago off western New Guinea, with a highly restricted range on this single island. It was described relatively recently and is considered near-threatened due to its tiny range.

children.name: Tanysiptera riedelii
children.description: A paradise kingfisher endemic to Biak and Supiori Islands in Cenderawasih Bay off northwestern New Guinea, with its restricted island range making it vulnerable to habitat disturbance. Males sport the characteristic elongated white central tail feathers of all paradise kingfishers.

children.name: Tanysiptera obiensis
children.description: A paradise kingfisher with a highly restricted range on Obi Island in the North Moluccas of Indonesia, isolated from related New Guinean species. Little is known of its ecology, and it is considered vulnerable due to deforestation of its limited island habitat.

children.name: Tanysiptera salvadorii
children.description: A paradise kingfisher from the islands of Yapen and Biak off the north coast of New Guinea, differing from the closely related Common Paradise Kingfisher in subtle plumage details and its more restricted range.

children.name: Tanysiptera rosseliana
children.description: A paradise kingfisher endemic to Rossel Island, the easternmost island of the Louisiade Archipelago in Milne Bay Province, Papua New Guinea. Its complete isolation on this small island means it remains very poorly known.

children.name: Tanysiptera meyeri
children.description: A paradise kingfisher endemic to Numfor Island in Cenderawasih Bay, northwestern New Guinea, with a very restricted range and closely related to the Common Paradise Kingfisher. The elongated white tail streamers are less extreme than in some relatives, and it remains poorly documented.

children.name: Tanysiptera nais
children.description: A paradise kingfisher from Biak Island off the north coast of New Guinea in Cenderawasih Bay, distinguished from the Common Paradise Kingfisher by its orange-rufous underparts and orange bill. It is the only paradise kingfisher endemic to Biak.

children.name: Actenoides
children.description: Jewel-kingfishers are brilliantly coloured, forest-dwelling kingfishers endemic to the islands of Southeast Asia, particularly the Philippines and Sulawesi. Secretive and rarely observed, they inhabit old-growth forest interior and feed on insects and lizards on the forest floor.

children.name: Actenoides lindsayi
children.description: A beautifully spotted, cryptically patterned kingfisher endemic to the Philippine islands of Luzon and Negros, living in primary montane forest. It is seldom observed due to its quiet, secretive nature and is assessed as near-threatened due to forest loss.

children.name: Actenoides concretus
children.description: A striking forest kingfisher of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo with an emerald green crown and back contrasting with a rich rufous collar and underparts in the male. It inhabits primary lowland and foothill rainforest and is vulnerable to deforestation.

children.name: Actenoides hombroni
children.description: A vulnerable, striking forest kingfisher endemic to Mindanao in the southern Philippines, with a vivid blue cap and green-spotted upperparts. It is known from very few specimens and sightings, confined to primary lowland and foothill forest.

children.name: Actenoides monachus
children.description: A beautifully patterned jewel-kingfisher endemic to Sulawesi and nearby islands, with green upperparts, a white collar, and rich chestnut-and-green spotted underparts. It inhabits primary and secondary forest and feeds on large insects, lizards, and earthworms on the forest floor.

children.name: Actenoides princeps
children.description: A little-known jewel-kingfisher of montane forests on Sulawesi, Indonesia, with a distinctive scaly pattern formed by the pale edges to each feather giving it an unusual appearance. It inhabits undisturbed hill and montane forest and is rarely encountered.

children.name: Actenoides bougainvillei
children.description: One of the rarest and most sought-after kingfishers in the world, restricted to the montane forests of Bougainville Island and Guadalcanal in the Solomons, known from fewer than ten confirmed sightings. The first confirmed photograph of a living individual was taken only in 2015.

children.name: Syma
children.description: Yellow-billed kingfishers are small, vividly coloured birds of New Guinean and Australian forests with a characteristic yellow-orange bill. They inhabit dense lowland forest and hunt insects and earthworms on the forest floor, nesting in arboreal termite mounds.

children.name: Syma torotoro
children.description: A small, round-headed forest kingfisher with a golden-yellow bill, orange head, and blue-green back, found across New Guinea and Cape York Peninsula in Australia. Its repeated, rising whistle is often heard in lowland rainforest long before the bird is seen.

children.name: Syma megarhyncha
children.description: A montane counterpart of the Yellow-billed Kingfisher, restricted to the mountains of New Guinea above 800 metres elevation. It is slightly larger with a proportionately bigger bill and replaces the Yellow-billed Kingfisher at higher altitudes in foothill and montane forest.

children.name: Todiramphus
children.description: A large genus of tree kingfishers distributed across the Pacific Islands, Southeast Asia, and Australasia. Many species are island endemics with extremely restricted ranges and are among the most threatened kingfishers, while others such as the Sacred Kingfisher are widespread and adaptable.

children.name: Todiramphus sanctus
children.description: Australia and New Zealand's most familiar kingfisher, breeding in woodland and mangroves before migrating north to winter in New Guinea and Indonesia. Its sharp, repeated 'kek-kek-kek' call is one of the emblematic sounds of the Australian bush.

children.name: Todiramphus chloris
children.description: The most widespread and geographically variable kingfisher in the world, ranging from the Red Sea coast across coastal Asia and the Pacific to Fiji. With around 50 recognised subspecies it is a classic example of an island-hopping species in rapid evolutionary diversification.

children.name: Todiramphus cinnamominus
children.description: Extinct in the wild since the 1980s following the devastation wrought by the invasive brown tree snake introduced to Guam, this cinnamon-and-turquoise kingfisher survives only in captive breeding programmes. Intensive conservation efforts aim to reintroduce it to snake-free islands.

children.name: Todiramphus macleayii
children.description: A vivid blue-and-white kingfisher of open woodland, forest edges, and gardens in northern and eastern Australia and New Guinea. It is a summer migrant to southern parts of its Australian range, hunting insects and small lizards from exposed perches.

children.name: Todiramphus pyrrhopygius
children.description: Australia's most arid-adapted kingfisher, found across dry inland scrub, spinifex grassland, and mulga woodland where it nests in termite mounds or earthen banks. The rusty-red rump and back contrast strikingly with its green head and white underparts.

children.name: Todiramphus australasia
children.description: A bright blue-and-cinnamon kingfisher of the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia, from Lombok east to Timor, inhabiting dry forest and woodland. The distinctive cinnamon wash on the underparts distinguishes it from the closely related Sacred Kingfisher.

children.name: Todiramphus nigrocyaneus
children.description: An unusual dark kingfisher of coastal and lowland forest in New Guinea and the western Papuan islands, with largely black-and-blue plumage unlike the typical patterning of most Todiramphus species. It is uncommon and associated with forest near water.

children.name: Todiramphus saurophagus
children.description: A large, bright white-and-blue kingfisher of coastal forests, mangroves, and offshore islands in the Moluccas, New Guinea, and the Bismarck Archipelago. Its name means 'lizard-eater' in Greek, reflecting its diet of lizards, crabs, and other coastal prey.

children.name: Todiramphus funebris
children.description: A distinctive, dark-plumaged kingfisher endemic to Halmahera in the North Moluccas of Indonesia, with a largely blackish head and mantle contrasting with blue wings. It is an uncommon resident of forest and forest edges on this single island.

children.name: Todiramphus recurvirostris
children.description: An unusual Samoan endemic kingfisher with a distinctly flattened, wide bill unlike typical Todiramphus species, possibly adapted for catching larger prey. It is found in forest and forest margins on Samoa and American Samoa.

children.name: Todiramphus albicilla
children.description: A Pacific island endemic kingfisher, closely related to the Collared Kingfisher but restricted to the Mariana Islands. Populations have suffered from habitat loss and invasive species pressures on the more developed islands of the group.

children.name: Todiramphus winchelli
children.description: A kingfisher of lowland forest and forest edges in the Philippines, distinguished by its warm rufous loral spot and rich colouration. It is found across several Philippine island groups and inhabits mangroves as well as inland forest.

children.name: Todiramphus albonotatus
children.description: A forest kingfisher endemic to New Britain in the Bismarck Archipelago of Papua New Guinea, characterised by white spots on the wings that distinguish it from related species. It inhabits lowland and hill forest and is rarely encountered.

children.name: Todiramphus leucopygius
children.description: A kingfisher of the Solomon Islands with a distinctive white rump and blue upperparts, inhabiting forest and secondary growth on the main islands. It is one of several Todiramphus species endemic to the Solomons archipelago.

children.name: Todiramphus farquhari
children.description: An endemic kingfisher of Vanuatu in the southwestern Pacific, with rich chestnut underparts distinguishing it from other island Todiramphus. It inhabits forest and forest edges on several islands of the Vanuatu chain.

children.name: Todiramphus bougainvillei
children.description: A kingfisher endemic to Manus Island in the Admiralty Islands of Papua New Guinea, with a restricted island range that makes it potentially vulnerable to forest clearance. It was for a long time treated as a subspecies of the Collared Kingfisher.

children.name: Todiramphus enigma
children.description: A little-studied kingfisher endemic to the Talaud Islands of northeastern Indonesia, known from very few specimens and described as 'enigmatic' by the ornithologists who first studied it. Its taxonomic status relative to other Todiramphus species is still debated.

children.name: Todiramphus gambieri
children.description: An extinct kingfisher formerly endemic to the Gambier Islands in French Polynesia, last recorded in the 19th century and presumably lost to deforestation and introduced predators. It is known only from historical museum specimens.

children.name: Todiramphus tutus
children.description: A small Todiramphus kingfisher endemic to the Cook Islands of the central Pacific, now restricted to Atiu and a few nearby islets following extinction from other Cook Island groups. It inhabits makatea (raised coral limestone) forest and is considered vulnerable.

children.name: Todiramphus lazuli
children.description: A small, bright azure-blue kingfisher endemic to Seram in the South Moluccas of Indonesia, distinguished by its particularly rich blue upperparts. It inhabits forest and forest margins and has a very restricted range on this single island.

children.name: Todiramphus pelewensis
children.description: A kingfisher endemic to the Palau archipelago of Micronesia in the western Pacific, with a restricted range across the main island group. It inhabits mangroves, forest margins, and open areas and is considered endangered due to its small total population.

children.name: Todiramphus diops
children.description: A small, blue-and-white kingfisher endemic to the Molucca Islands of eastern Indonesia, particularly Halmahera, Bacan, and Obi. It inhabits forest edges and secondary growth and is often placed near the Collared Kingfisher complex.

children.name: Todiramphus colonus
children.description: A widespread island kingfisher of the Bismarck Archipelago, Solomon Islands, and Vanuatu, formerly lumped within the Collared Kingfisher complex but now treated as a separate species on morphological and distributional grounds. It inhabits mangroves, forest edges, and secondary vegetation.

children.name: Todiramphus tristrami
children.description: A forest kingfisher endemic to the Solomon Islands, differing from related Collared Kingfisher populations in having a darker, more extensive black head mask. It is found on several of the larger Solomon islands and inhabits forest interior.

children.name: Todiramphus veneratus
children.description: A kingfisher endemic to the Society Islands of French Polynesia including Tahiti and Moorea, with a restricted insular range and considered near-threatened. It inhabits forest and modified habitats including gardens and roadsides.

children.name: Todiramphus ruficollaris
children.description: A critically endangered kingfisher endemic to the small island of Mangaia in the southern Cook Islands, with an estimated total population of fewer than 200 individuals. It is threatened by habitat degradation and was the last Cook Islands kingfisher to receive formal species recognition.

children.name: Todiramphus gertrudae
children.description: A kingfisher restricted to the Louisiade Archipelago and D'Entrecasteaux Islands of Milne Bay Province, Papua New Guinea, recently elevated from a subspecies of the Collared Kingfisher. It inhabits coastal forest, mangroves, and forest edges on several islands in the group.

children.name: Todiramphus vitianus
children.description: A kingfisher endemic to the Fiji archipelago, one of the outermost members of the Todiramphus chloris group in the Pacific, inhabiting forest, mangroves, and coastal scrub on multiple Fijian islands. It is assessed as near-threatened due to invasive species pressures.

children.name: Halcyon
children.description: A diverse genus of tree kingfishers spanning Africa and Asia, characterised by large, hooked bills, often brilliant blue-and-rufous or white-and-blue plumage, and loud, far-carrying calls. Many species are far from water and hunt insects, lizards, and small vertebrates in woodland and savanna.

children.name: Halcyon smyrnensis
children.description: One of the most familiar and adaptable kingfishers of South and Southeast Asia, equally at home in cities, farmland, and forest as beside water. Its brilliant turquoise-blue back and chestnut flanks contrast with a bright white throat and breast.

children.name: Halcyon coromanda
children.description: A richly coloured rufous kingfisher with a violet wash on its back, found in dense lowland forests and mangroves across East and Southeast Asia. A partial migrant, it breeds in Japan and northeastern Asia before wintering in the tropics.

children.name: Halcyon pileata
children.description: A striking kingfisher with a glossy black crown, white collar, and vivid blue back breeding in East Asia and wintering across South and Southeast Asia along rivers, estuaries, and mangroves. It feeds on fish, crabs, and large insects.

children.name: Halcyon senegalensis
children.description: A bold, blue-and-white kingfisher of sub-Saharan African woodland and savanna whose loud, trilling calls are a defining sound of the African wet season. Despite its name, it rarely fishes, preferring insects, lizards, and small frogs caught on the ground.

children.name: Halcyon leucocephala
children.description: A chestnut-bellied kingfisher with a grey head and brilliant blue wings ranging across sub-Saharan Africa and the Cape Verde Islands, with a population wintering on the Arabian Peninsula. It inhabits dry woodland, bush, and savanna, hunting insects and small vertebrates.

children.name: Halcyon malimbica
children.description: A large forest kingfisher of West and Central African rainforest with deep sky-blue upperparts and a distinctive blue wash across the breast. It perches quietly in the mid-storey of forest interior, sallying to the ground for large insects and small vertebrates.

children.name: Halcyon chelicuti
children.description: A small, subtly marked kingfisher of open woodland and savanna across sub-Saharan Africa, distinctive among its genus for its streaked brown head and understated plumage. It is entirely terrestrial in its hunting habits, catching insects and lizards from low perches.

children.name: Halcyon albiventris
children.description: A common and widespread kingfisher of eastern and southern African woodland, forest margins, and gardens, recognised by its brown head, streaked breast, and vivid blue back. It hunts insects, earthworms, small lizards, and occasionally fish.

children.name: Halcyon badia
children.description: A striking forest kingfisher of West and Central African rainforest with a rich chocolate-brown back and bright blue rump and wings contrasting with white underparts. It is a shy bird of forest interior, hunting insects from low perches.

children.name: Halcyon cyanoventris
children.description: A beautifully coloured kingfisher endemic to Java and Bali in Indonesia, with a deep violet-blue belly contrasting with a chestnut head and brilliant blue back. It inhabits lowland and foothill forest and is classified as near-threatened due to deforestation.

children.name: Halcyon fulgidus
children.description: A moderately large tree kingfisher of the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia, with rich cinnamon underparts and blue upperparts. It inhabits forest and forest edges on Lombok, Sumbawa, Flores, and adjacent islands.

children.name: Halcyon torotoro
children.description: A widespread tree kingfisher of New Guinea and nearby islands, with yellow-orange underparts, a blue crown, and white collar, inhabiting forest and forest edges from the lowlands to mid-montane elevations. It is one of the most commonly encountered kingfishers in New Guinea.

children.name: Halcyon senegaloides
children.description: A large, blue-and-orange tree kingfisher closely related to the Woodland Kingfisher but specialising in mangrove, coastal forest, and riverine habitats along the east coast of Africa. It has a distinctive all-black bill that separates it from the Woodland Kingfisher.

children.name: Halcyon winifredae
children.description: A little-known tree kingfisher restricted to montane forest in the northern Philippines on Luzon, known from very few specimens and rarely observed. Its montane forest habitat is under increasing pressure from logging and agricultural conversion.

children.name: Halcyon isabellina
children.description: A poorly-known insular tree kingfisher endemic to the Tanimbar Islands in the southern Moluccas of Indonesia, with pale, washed-out plumage that differs from the richer-coloured mainland Halcyon species. It was long treated as a subspecies of the Ruddy Kingfisher before being elevated to full species status.

children.name: Lacedo
children.description: A monotypic genus of Southeast Asian forest kingfishers, the Banded Kingfisher being a strikingly barred, sexually dichromatic species of lowland tropical forest. Males and females differ so dramatically in plumage that they were once described as separate species.

children.name: Lacedo pulchella
children.description: A beautifully barred kingfisher of lowland and foothill rainforests from the Malay Peninsula to Borneo and Sumatra, with males showing rufous, black, and white banding quite unlike any other kingfisher. It hunts insects and lizards in the forest interior, perching quietly at mid-height.

children.name: Pelargopsis
children.description: Stork-billed kingfishers are very large, big-headed tree kingfishers with massive red bills characteristic of coastal Asia, favouring mangroves, large rivers, and forested coastlines. They are among the largest members of the family and are primarily fish-eaters.

children.name: Pelargopsis capensis
children.description: One of the largest kingfishers in the world, with a disproportionately huge red bill used to catch large fish, frogs, and crabs along the rivers, mangroves, and coastal forests of South and Southeast Asia. It is a noisy, conspicuous bird that calls raucously when disturbed.

children.name: Pelargopsis amauroptera
children.description: A near-threatened, large kingfisher with distinctive brown wings contrasting with its orange head and body, restricted to mangrove forests along the coasts of Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, and the Andaman Islands. Specialised mangrove habitat loss poses the primary threat to this species.

children.name: Pelargopsis melanorhyncha
children.description: A large stork-billed kingfisher endemic to Sulawesi and surrounding islands in Indonesia, distinguished from its relatives by its distinctive black bill rather than the red bill typical of the genus. It inhabits rivers, forest streams, and coastal mangroves and is rarely observed.

children.name: Megaceryle
children.description: Large, boldly patterned kingfishers occurring on rivers, coasts, and lakes across the Americas, Africa, and Asia. All members of this genus are conspicuous, noisy hunters that hover before plunging or dive from elevated perches.

children.name: Megaceryle alcyon
children.description: North America's most widespread kingfisher, a loud rattling presence along virtually any water body from Alaska to Panama. Females are unusually more colourful than males, bearing a chestnut belly-band in addition to the blue-grey breast band.

children.name: Megaceryle torquata
children.description: The largest kingfisher of the Americas, ranging from the lower Rio Grande through Central and South America to Tierra del Fuego. It favours large rivers and lakes, hovering noisily before plummeting into deep water.

children.name: Megaceryle maxima
children.description: Africa's largest kingfisher, a hefty, shaggy-crested bird that perches prominently on rocks and branches along rivers, streams, and estuaries. It hunts crabs and large fish, plunging from height or hovering briefly before striking.

children.name: Megaceryle lugubris
children.description: A large, boldly barred black-and-white kingfisher of fast-flowing Himalayan and East Asian rivers, equipped with a distinctive shaggy crest. It is an indicator of healthy, clear mountain watercourses and is sensitive to pollution.

children.name: Ceryle
children.description: Pied kingfishers are the sole member of this genus, renowned for their habit of hovering in stationary flight over water before diving for fish — a strategy that frees them from dependence on riverside perches and allows them to exploit open lakes and coastlines.

children.name: Ceryle rudis
children.description: The world's most widespread kingfisher and the only one that routinely hovers over open water to locate prey without a perch. Highly gregarious, it often nests colonially and helpers assist breeding pairs at the nest.

children.name: Chloroceryle
children.description: Green-and-white American kingfishers occupying a range of tropical and subtropical aquatic habitats from Mexico to Argentina. They occupy distinct ecological niches by size, from the large Amazon Kingfisher down to the tiny American Pygmy Kingfisher.

children.name: Chloroceryle amazona
children.description: The largest of the green kingfishers, found along rivers, lakes, and mangroves from Mexico to northern Argentina. It hunts large fish by perching motionless over the water and plunging with precision.

children.name: Chloroceryle americana
children.description: A small, inconspicuous kingfisher of shaded streams, rivers, and ponds throughout the Americas from the southern United States to central Argentina. It perches low near the water's edge and darts out to catch small fish.

children.name: Chloroceryle aenea
children.description: The smallest kingfisher in the Americas, barely larger than a hummingbird, found in dense tropical lowland forest along small shaded streams and mangrove channels. It hunts tiny fish and aquatic invertebrates with fast, direct dives.

children.name: Chloroceryle inda
children.description: A mid-sized, richly rufous-bellied kingfisher of heavily forested tropical streams, swamps, and flooded forest from Honduras to Bolivia and Brazil. Secretive and easily overlooked, it favours dark, densely vegetated watercourses.
